00.01.title: From Nand to Tetris: Course Projects
00.01.use: Use the twelve-project dependency chain to refine the workbook sketch from terminal output down through software, ISA, memory, and logic boundaries.

00.01.title: MIT 6.004 Computation Structures
00.01.use: Use the course sequence to populate the trace-vocabulary table with concrete units of progress from gates through processors, devices, and systems.

00.01.title: Pro Git: About Version Control
00.01.use: Use this when defining clean baselines and reversible checkpoints for the homework's deliberate validation failure and restoration.

00.02.title: MIT Missing Semester: Course Overview and the Shell
00.02.use: Use the shell, quoting, redirection, and process examples while predicting commands and separating output, diagnostics, and exit status.

00.02.title: MIT Missing Semester: Debugging and Profiling
00.02.use: Use its debugger workflow to turn the deliberately broken homework program into a reproducible causal trace rather than a symptom report.

00.02.title: GCC Warning Options
00.02.use: Look up every strict-build warning before changing code, especially conversion, signedness, return-type, and unused-value diagnostics.

00.02.title: GCC Instrumentation Options
00.02.use: Use the AddressSanitizer and UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer sections to build and interpret the required out-of-bounds failure trace.

00.03.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Types III — Conversions
00.03.use: Use the integer-promotion, signed/unsigned-conversion, and strtol sections to trace the workbook expressions and reject trailing input.

00.03.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Fixed-Width Integer Types
00.03.use: Use this to complete the host range table and distinguish exact-width types from least-width, fast, and ordinary integer types.

00.03.title: SEI CERT C INT30-C: Ensure Unsigned Integer Operations Do Not Wrap
00.03.use: Apply its precondition patterns to offset-plus-count validation and every size calculation in the field decoder.

00.03.title: SEI CERT C INT34-C: Validate Shift Counts
00.03.use: Use its operand-width rules to justify the decoder's width-32 special case and every rejected shift boundary.

00.04.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Bitwise Operations
00.04.use: Use the AND, OR, XOR, and shift examples before deriving masks and byte contributions in the workbook.

00.04.title: WG14 N1570 C Committee Draft
00.04.use: Consult sections 6.2.6.1, 6.5.7, and 7.20.1.1 for object representation, shift semantics, and exact-width integer guarantees.

00.04.title: Linux Kernel Documentation: Unaligned Memory Accesses
00.04.use: Use the architecture examples to explain precisely why casting an arbitrary byte pointer to uint32_t pointer is not a portable decoder.

00.04.title: GNU Coreutils: od Invocation
00.04.use: Use od as an independent byte-level oracle for the homework utility's offsets, hexadecimal bytes, character view, and endianness tests.

00.05.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Pointers
00.05.use: Use its address, dereference, and const examples to draw the workbook object diagrams before implementing the bounded buffer.

00.05.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Arrays
00.05.use: Use the array-to-pointer and function-parameter sections to explain the differing sizeof results and why a parameter cannot prove the supplied bound.

00.05.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Strings
00.05.use: Use its terminator and storage examples when implementing exact-fit text copies, embedded-zero tests, and missing-terminator failures.

00.05.title: SEI CERT C ARR30-C: Do Not Form or Use Out-of-Bounds Pointers
00.05.use: Apply its pointer-validity cases to the cursor invariant, one-past pointer discussion, and attempted read-past-end tests.

00.06.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Structs
00.06.use: Use the struct, copying, comparison, and padding discussion before measuring offsets and writing the collection invariant.

00.06.title: Beej's Guide to C Programming: Manual Memory Allocation
00.06.use: Use its malloc, realloc, failure-checking, and free examples to stage the owned collection implementation.

00.06.title: SEI CERT C MEM35-C: Allocate Sufficient Memory for an Object
00.06.use: Apply its sizeof and multiplication guards to every append allocation and forced-allocation-failure case.

00.06.title: SEI CERT C MEM31-C: Free Dynamically Allocated Memory When No Longer Needed
00.06.use: Use it to audit the constructor failure matrix and prove that destroy releases every owned allocation exactly once.

00.07.title: MIT 6.004: Procedures and Stacks
00.07.use: Use the calling-convention and activation-record material to annotate arguments, saved state, return values, and stack movement in the workbook call trace.

00.07.title: ELF Object File Format
00.07.use: Consult chapters 3, 5, 6, and 7 when classifying sections, symbols, relocations, and loadable segments in the object-file report.

00.07.title: GNU Binary Utilities: readelf
00.07.use: Use readelf to capture section headers, symbol tables, and relocations before and after the final link.

00.07.title: GNU Binary Utilities: objdump
00.07.use: Use objdump to follow one call through argument setup and return, then compare debug and optimized disassembly.

00.08.title: MIT 6.004: Combinational Logic
00.08.use: Use the Boolean, mux, and combinational-circuit material to derive the workbook truth tables and LUT masks.

00.08.title: MIT 6.004: Sequential Logic
00.08.use: Use its clocked-state model to keep current value, computed next value, edge, and observed value separate in counter traces.

00.08.title: MIT 6.004: Finite State Machines
00.08.use: Use the FSM construction method for the IDLE-DATA-DONE workbook table and the two-edge request-controller homework.

01.01.title: MIT 6.004: The Digital Abstraction
01.01.use: Use this to state what logic levels preserve and what analog voltage, timing, noise, and manufacturing details the lab deliberately hides.

01.01.title: MIT 6.004: CMOS
01.01.use: Use the MOS switch networks to justify the abstraction-map steps from controlled switch through inverter and logic gates.

01.01.title: Nand2Tetris Project 1: Boolean Logic
01.01.use: Use its staged gate and multiplexer construction as extra practical work after completing the exhaustive C LUT properties.

01.01.title: AMD UltraScale Architecture Configurable Logic Block User Guide UG574
01.01.use: Read the Look-Up Table and Storage Elements sections to connect the lab's abstract mask and counter to real FPGA LUT and register resources.

01.02.title: cocotb Documentation: The Timing Model
01.02.use: Use simulator phases and triggers to repair the workbook test that changes an input at the same timestamp as the rising edge.

01.02.title: Verilator Guide: Trace Options
01.02.use: Use the VCD or FST trace options when implementing the optional bounded harness and capturing the first divergent cycle.

01.02.title: GTKWave Documentation
01.02.use: Use it to load the generated trace, select only the clock-reset-state-output cone, and annotate the deliberate defect.

02.01.title: Nand2Tetris Project 3: Memory
02.01.use: Use its register and counter exercises to reinforce edge-triggered state before writing the configurable LED-divider step model.

02.01.title: Intel Quartus Prime: Counter HDL Guidelines
02.01.use: Use the counter coding patterns to review the optional RTL implementation after the portable reference model passes.

02.01.title: Intel Quartus Prime: Use Synchronous Resets
02.01.use: Use its reset timing discussion to specify exactly what repeated assertion and between-edge deassertion mean in the homework trace.

02.01.title: geohot/fromthetransistor realhw branch snapshot
02.01.use: Treat the partial Spartan-6 LED synthesis and SPI-flash exploration as a historical bring-up trace: identify unstated board, tool, reset, protocol, and validation assumptions, then replace them with the course's portable model, exact-cycle tests, and evidence manifest.

02.02.title: OpenTitan UART: Theory of Operation
02.02.use: Use its frame diagram and 16x receive sampling sequence to check the 8N1 encodings, sample edges, framing errors, and overrun model.

02.02.title: OpenTitan UART: Register Reference
02.02.use: Use the documented reset values and register side effects as a model for the homework's DATA and STATUS MMIO table.

02.02.title: WG14 N1570 C Committee Draft
02.02.use: Read section 6.7.3 on volatile-qualified objects before explaining what volatile MMIO accesses guarantee and what ordering or atomicity they do not.

03.01.title: Nand2Tetris Project 6: The Assembler
03.01.use: Use its staged symbol-free then symbolic implementation and independent comparison tool as the model for the FTT-16 two-pass assembler.

03.01.title: RISC-V RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set
03.01.use: Study sections 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 to compare fixed fields, immediate reconstruction, reserved encodings, and signed control-flow offsets with FTT-16.

03.01.title: GNU Binutils: Using as
03.01.use: Use the Syntax, Symbols, Labels, and Expressions chapters when adding comments and labels without weakening operand validation.

03.02.title: Nand2Tetris Project 5: Computer Architecture
03.02.use: Use its CPU-memory-program tests to plan incremental FTT-16 emulator tests before combining all instruction families.

03.02.title: RISC-V RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set
03.02.use: Use the programmer's model and arithmetic, control-transfer, load, and store semantics as an example of separating architectural state from instruction encoding.

03.02.title: Sail RISC-V Formal ISA Model
03.02.use: Study how encoders, decoders, instruction semantics, and an executable emulator derive from one precise model when designing an independent FTT-16 oracle.

03.03.title: MCUboot Serial Recovery
03.03.use: Use its length, framing, integrity, buffer, and timeout tradeoffs to design truncation recovery and resynchronization tests for the boot protocol.

03.03.title: U-Boot loadb Command
03.03.use: Use this real monitor command as a comparison point for serial image reception, destination bounds, transfer results, and later execution.

03.03.title: MCUboot Bootloader Design
03.03.use: Use its magic, little-endian headers, image slots, validation, RAM loading, and chain-loading flow to review the monitor's validate-then-commit invariant.

04.01.title: My First Language Frontend with LLVM Tutorial
04.01.use: Use chapters 1-3 and 5 to structure the lexer, recursive-descent parser, AST, code generation, and control-flow milestones; adapt the architecture to C0 and direct assembly output.

04.01.title: Clang CFE Internals Manual
04.01.use: Use its source-location, token, parser, AST, and diagnostic-testing sections to implement precise spans, deterministic errors, and malformed-input fixtures.

04.01.title: WG14 N2176: C17 Draft
04.01.use: Use clauses 6.4, 6.5, and 6.8 to freeze lexical rules, precedence, short-circuit behavior, and statements for the supported C0 subset.

04.01.title: chibicc
04.01.use: After completing each stage independently, compare its incremental tokenizer, parser, type checker, and code-generator boundaries and test organization.

04.02.title: GNU ld Manual
04.02.use: Use the linker-script, SECTIONS, alignment, symbol, and map-file chapters as a concrete model for section merging, address assignment, and deterministic map output.

04.02.title: System V ABI: ELF Object File Format
04.02.use: Use the ELF header, section table, string table, symbol table, relocation, and program-header definitions to derive bounded parsing and relocation formulas.

04.02.title: GNU readelf Manual
04.02.use: Use readelf header, section, symbol, relocation, and segment views as an external oracle for linker acceptance tests.

04.03.title: Stanford CS107 Assignment 6: Heap Allocator
04.03.use: Follow its progression from bump allocation to implicit and explicit free lists, splitting, coalescing, heap validation, scripted traces, and utilization measurement.

04.03.title: Embed with GNU: libgloss
04.03.use: Use its crt0, linker-script, I/O-hook, and allocation-support guidance to connect startup, BSS/data initialization, stack setup, and the freestanding runtime.

04.03.title: WG14 N2176: C17 Draft
04.03.use: Use the freestanding-environment, allocation, string, and byte-operation clauses to define contracts and host differential tests.

04.03.title: musl String Function Sources
04.03.use: After writing your own routines, compare small production implementations of memcpy, memmove, memcmp, strlen, and related functions, especially overlap and byte semantics.

04.04.title: RFC 9542: IANA Considerations and IETF Protocol Usage for IEEE 802 Parameters
04.04.use: Use its Ethernet MAC and EtherType layout plus local experimental EtherTypes 0x88B5 and 0x88B6 to build endian-explicit frame fixtures.

04.04.title: IEEE 802.3-2022 Ethernet Standard
04.04.use: Use the MAC clauses as the normative reference for frame fields, permitted sizes, padding, and FCS while keeping the exercise scoped to a small controller model.

04.04.title: Linux Dynamic DMA Mapping Guide
04.04.use: Use its ownership, DMA-direction, synchronization, and memory-barrier rules when implementing descriptor-ring and packet-buffer lifecycles.

04.04.title: Intel Ethernet Controller I210 Datasheet
04.04.use: Study its TX/RX descriptor rings, head and tail registers, completion bits, and interrupt cause/acknowledgement behavior as a concrete comparison for the simulated MMIO device.

04.05.title: RFC 1350: The TFTP Protocol
04.05.use: Use its compact transfer packet and state model to practice block numbering, duplicate handling, acknowledgement, retransmission, and timeout behavior; document where the course protocol permits reordering.

04.05.title: MCUboot Bootloader Design
04.05.use: Use its image headers, bounded slots, integrity checks, resumable updates, and boot handoff model to design verify-before-commit semantics.

04.05.title: U-Boot Verified Boot
04.05.use: Use it to distinguish corruption-detecting hashes from authenticated signatures and to define checked image metadata and handoff conditions.

04.05.title: U-Boot Emulation of Network Devices
04.05.use: Use its emulated-network and TFTP setup patterns to create a sandboxed localhost fixture for loss, duplication, reordering, corruption, and timeout replays.

05.01.title: xv6: A Simple, Unix-like Teaching Operating System
05.01.use: Read the page-table chapter before implementing the lab; draw VPN, PPN, and offset decomposition and trace map, unmap, permission, and exec-mapping lifecycles.

05.01.title: MIT 6.1810 Page Tables Lab
05.01.use: Use its PTE inspection and user-mapping exercises as practice before building the reference page walk, TLB, and differential trace harness.

05.01.title: RISC-V Supervisor-Level ISA
05.01.use: Use the Sv32, PTE, satp, ASID, page-fault, accessed/dirty-bit, and SFENCE.VMA sections for exact walk, permission, and invalidation behavior.

05.02.title: xv6: A Simple, Unix-like Teaching Operating System
05.02.use: Use the traps, system calls, page tables, processes, scheduling, sleep/wakeup, file descriptors, and file-system chapters as the conceptual spine for the OS simulator.

05.02.title: MIT 6.1810 System Calls Lab
05.02.use: Use its user stub, trap, dispatch, process-state, tracing, and debugger workflow to practice the complete syscall path before extending your own system.

05.02.title: POSIX.1-2024 fork
05.02.use: Use it to specify parent and child return values, copied descriptor tables, shared open-file descriptions, independent process lifetimes, and failure cases.

05.02.title: POSIX.1-2024 exec
05.02.use: Use its preserved and replaced process attributes, argv rules, failure semantics, and success-does-not-return contract to test atomic exec.

05.03.title: Virtual I/O Device Specification 1.3: Block Device
05.03.use: Use the block-device section's sector, operation, data, status, read, write, flush, and completion model as inspiration for the course request/result API.

05.03.title: SD Association Simplified Specifications
05.03.use: Use the Physical Layer Simplified Specification's command, response, state, and initialization diagrams to model a small card state machine that rejects data operations before readiness.

05.03.title: POSIX.1-2024 read and pread
05.03.use: Use positional-read, EOF, zero-length, short-transfer, and interruption rules to define a safe file-backed sector adapter.

05.03.title: Linux Fault Injection Infrastructure
05.03.use: Use its every-nth failure, block-I/O failure, timeout, and deterministic configuration patterns to design seeded request faults and reproducible replays.

05.04.title: FAT Filesystem
05.04.use: Use its byte diagrams and formulas to derive FAT16 BPB geometry, FAT entries, directory records, cluster chains, and cluster-to-sector translation by hand before coding.

05.04.title: UEFI Specification 2.10: Media Access
05.04.use: Use the FAT12/16/32 BPB and block-I/O sections to validate boot geometry, directory structure, media boundaries, and interoperability assumptions.

05.04.title: ECMA-107: Volume and File Structure of Disk Cartridges for Information Interchange
05.04.use: Use its normative 12-bit and 16-bit FAT allocation, defective-space, descriptor, and short-name rules when defining the supported FAT16 profile.

05.04.title: The Sleuth Kit FAT Documentation
05.04.use: Use its forensic descriptions and tools as an independent oracle for boot sectors, FAT copies, directory entries, deletion markers, cluster chains, and slack-space corruption cases.

05.05.title: MIT 6.1810 Xv6 and Unix Utilities Lab
05.05.use: Use the sleep, find, xargs, primes, and pipe exercises to practice small C utilities plus fork, exec, wait, pipe, and correct file-descriptor closure.

05.05.title: MIT xv6-riscv
05.05.use: Study user/init.c, user/sh.c, user/cat.c, user/ls.c, and user/rm.c as compact references for reaping, command execution, redirection, pipelines, and robust I/O loops.

05.05.title: POSIX.1-2024 Shell Command Language
05.05.use: Use the token, command, redirection, pipeline, environment, and built-in sections to freeze the shell's deliberately narrow grammar and explicitly list unsupported features.

05.05.title: Buildroot User Manual
05.05.use: Use its init-system discussion to compare PID 1, service startup, and boot sequencing with the course init process and boot transcript.

06.01.title: RFC 9293: Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)
06.01.use: Use the free current TCP base specification for the state variables, transition model, sequence and acknowledgment rules, receive-window checks, handshake, data transfer, and FIN traces in the workbook.

06.01.title: RFC 6298: Computing TCP's Retransmission Timer
06.01.use: Use its timer start, restart, expiry, retransmit-earliest, and exponential-backoff rules to design the simulated-clock loss and lost-ACK tests; full text is free.

06.01.title: RFC 1071: Computing the Internet Checksum
06.01.use: Use the worked one's-complement arithmetic, odd-byte handling, end-around carry, and byte-order discussion to implement and hand-check checksum fixtures; this is a free implementation memo, not a normative standard.

06.02.title: RFC 854: Telnet Protocol Specification
06.02.use: Use the free protocol text to build the streaming IAC command parser, WILL/WONT/DO/DONT refusal behavior, escaped-IAC handling, and CR-LF versus CR-NUL tests across every split boundary.

06.02.title: fork(2) — Linux manual page
06.02.use: Use the inherited-file-descriptor and separate-address-space rules to draw parent and session-child ownership and explain why every unowned socket copy must be closed.

06.02.title: dup(2) — Linux manual page
06.02.use: Use dup2 semantics to connect a session socket or pipes to child standard input and output while testing aliasing, close ordering, EOF, and failed handoff paths.

06.02.title: poll(2) — Linux manual page
06.02.use: Use readiness, POLLHUP, error, timeout, and partial-I/O behavior to prevent one slow client from blocking another and to force deterministic concurrent interleavings.

06.03.title: ELF Object File Format, Chapter 8: Dynamic Linking
06.03.use: Use PT_INTERP, the dynamic array, DT_NEEDED ordering, breadth-first dependency scope, map-once behavior, GOT/PLT concepts, and relocation-before-control-transfer as the loader's phase contract; full text is free.

06.03.title: ELF Object File Format, Chapter 7: Program Loading
06.03.use: Use PT_LOAD file-versus-memory ranges, alignment, zero-fill, and segment permission rules to validate mappings and derive final non-writable executable regions.

06.03.title: ELF Object File Format, Chapter 6: Relocation
06.03.use: Use REL versus RELA, relocation offsets, symbol indices, addends, relative relocation factors, and bounded target writes when implementing the course's documented relocation formulas.

06.03.title: Dynamic Linker Environment Variables — The GNU C Library
06.03.use: Use LD_DEBUG categories such as libs, reloc, symbols, bindings, and scopes to obtain a real loader trace and compare it with the learner loader's dependency, lookup, and relocation trace.

06.04.title: RFC 9112: HTTP/1.1
06.04.use: Use the free current HTTP/1.1 message-syntax and body-length rules to implement incremental status/header parsing, Content-Length, chunked framing, connection-close framing, and conflicting-framing rejection.

06.04.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics
06.04.use: Use methods, status codes, fields, Content-Length semantics, Location, redirects, and HTTP URI rules to define exactly what the bounded client accepts and follows.

06.04.title: WHATWG HTML Standard: Parsing HTML documents
06.04.use: Use selected tokenizer states, character references, parse-error behavior, and tree-construction concepts to specify the constrained streaming subset and malformed fixtures without attempting the entire browser algorithm.

06.04.title: WHATWG URL Standard
06.04.use: Use basic parsing, relative-reference resolution, path shortening, serialization, and scheme handling to implement link normalization while explicitly rejecting every scheme outside the course subset.

07.01.title: IEEE Std 1149.1 (JTAG) Testability Primer
07.01.use: Use this free TI primer for the TAP state diagram, capture-shift-update separation, instruction and data registers, BYPASS, IDCODE, bit flow, and hand-written scan transcripts.

07.01.title: OpenOCD User's Guide: JTAG Commands
07.01.use: Use irscan, drscan, pathmove, stable-state names, BYPASS behavior, and explicit end states to compare the simulated navigator with a real JTAG tool's operation model.

07.01.title: The RISC-V Debug Specification
07.01.use: Use its JTAG Debug Transport Module, IDCODE, BYPASS, reset, and error-response material as a concrete modern target when extending transcript and fault-diagnosis tests.

07.01.title: IEEE 1149.1-2013: IEEE Standard for Test Access Port and Boundary-Scan Architecture
07.01.use: Use this as the authoritative published-standard record; the full text requires purchase or subscription, and IEEE marks this edition inactive-reserved while a revision project is active, so the TI and OpenOCD sources are the practical free readings.

07.02.title: Digilent Arty A7-100 Master XDC
07.02.use: Use this official supported-board constraint file as the concrete manifest fixture for package pins, I/O standards, clock constraints, UART, storage, Ethernet, and expansion signals; replace it with the matching vendor file if another board is selected.

07.02.title: 7 Series FPGAs PCB Design Guide (UG483)
07.02.use: Use the official vendor guide to review power-distribution networks, rail requirements, decoupling, return paths, stackup, and signal-integrity assumptions behind the manifest and BOM checklist.

07.02.title: 7 Series FPGAs SelectIO Resources User Guide (UG471)
07.02.use: Use bank capabilities, VCCO requirements, I/O-standard compatibility, drive, termination, and clock-capable-pin rules to explain every accepted and rejected pin assignment.

07.02.title: 7 Series FPGAs Configuration User Guide (UG470)
07.02.use: Use configuration modes, startup sequencing, mode pins, PROGRAM_B, INIT_B, DONE, configuration memory, boundary scan, and JTAG access to audit whether the board remains recoverable before application logic works.

07.03.title: Digilent Arty A7 Revision E.2 Schematic
07.03.use: Use this official board schematic to turn the bring-up ladder into exact rail, ground, clock, reset, configuration, JTAG, UART, DDR3, flash, and Ethernet checkpoints with named test nodes and expected connectivity.

07.03.title: Vivado Design Suite User Guide: Programming and Debugging (UG908)
07.03.use: Use the current Hardware Manager, target connection, device programming, debug-probe, and diagnostic flows to identify the JTAG chain, load a known-good bitstream, and save reproducible programming evidence.

07.03.title: Vivado Design Suite Tutorial: Programming and Debugging (UG936)
07.03.use: Use the official ILA and VIO exercises to capture stable internal events, compare logic traces with the simulator, and localize the first semantic divergence without changing several variables at once.

07.03.title: Zynq 7000 SoC and 7 Series Devices Memory Interface Solutions User Guide (UG586)
07.03.use: Use initialization, calibration-complete, example traffic generator, address mapping, timing-window, and interface-debug material before interpreting walking-bit or address-alias RAM failures.

capstone.title: Reproducible Builds Documentation
capstone.use: Use the free project guidance to record source revision, toolchain, environment, paths, timestamps, and build inputs so the single capstone command can be reproduced from a fresh checkout.

capstone.title: RFC 9112: HTTP/1.1
capstone.use: Use message framing as the transport-to-parser boundary contract when proving that every tested byte split produces the same parsed response and terminal snapshot.

capstone.title: WHATWG HTML Standard: Parsing HTML documents
capstone.use: Use selected tokenizer and malformed-input rules to design the unclosed-construct fault, record the first divergent token state, and state precisely how the bounded learner subset differs from a production browser.

capstone.title: Clang AddressSanitizer
capstone.use: Use a separate host acceptance build with AddressSanitizer to catch out-of-bounds access, use-after-free, double-free, and leaks during the fault campaign; do not treat it as the final static-linked artifact because the documented runtime does not support static linking.
